Title: The function of PROTOPORPHYRINOGEN IX OXIDASE in chlorophyll biosynthesis requires oxidised plastoquinone in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Jex4; ptox2; ptox2 petB; ptox2-R petB
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plastid terminal oxidase; di-iron quinol oxidase
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PTOX2 absence contributes to over-reduction of the plastoquinone pool, indirectly impairing PPX function and chlorophyll biosynthesis in the ptox2 petB background
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PTOX2 is the major oxidase involved in chlororespiration in C. reinhardtii. 2. Loss of PTOX2 together with loss of PetB causes near-complete reduction of the PQ pool in light. 3. Reintroduction of wild-type PTOX2 rescues the ptox2 petB phenotype, reducing Proto accumulation-associated defects.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant and complementation analysis; immunoblotting; chlorophyll fluorescence; metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: PSAD strong constitutive promoter used for complementation construct
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In the double mutant lacking PTOX2 and PetB, cells show severe light sensitivity and pigment defects
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining PTOX2-supported PQ oxidation may be important when engineering chlorophyll/tetrapyrrole metabolism under fluctuating redox conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PTOX2 itself is not a tetrapyrrole pathway enzyme but is a core experimental gene because its loss creates the redox state that reveals PPX dependence on oxidized PQ.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytochrome b6 subunit of cytochrome b6f complex
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PetB blocks cytochrome b6f formation and, together with PTOX2 loss, causes plastoquinone over-reduction that indirectly disrupts PPX-dependent Proto synthesis for chlorophyll biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. petB mutants lack cytochrome b6f and cannot grow photoautotrophically. 2. petB alone does not cause the extreme Proto accumulation seen in ptox2 petB. 3. In combination with ptox2, petB creates the highly reduced PQ state that reveals PPX dependence on oxidized PQ.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; immunoblotting of cyt b6 and cyt f; chlorophyll fluorescence; growth assays; metabolite profiling
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: cyt f; cytochrome b6f complex components
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes non-photosynthetic phenotype and inability to grow on TP medium
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Demonstrates that electron transport bottlenecks can strongly perturb chlorophyll biosynthesis via PQ redox effects
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: petB is a core experimental gene in the double-mutant system but acts indirectly on tetrapyrrole metabolism through photosynthetic electron transport rather than direct catalysis in the pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Jex4; ptox2; petB; ptox2 petB; ptox2-R petB
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Tetrapyrrole biosynthesis / chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Last common step before the heme/chlorophyll branch point
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal common step before branch point
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Chlorophyll is essential for photosynthesis; heme is essential for cellular energetic metabolism including photosynthesis and respiration
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX by removing six electrons and protons; the study supports oxidized plastoquinone as the electron acceptor in vivo
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the common precursor for both chlorophyll and heme branches
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light; DCMU treatment; oxyfluorfen treatment; over-reduced plastoquinone pool in ptox2 petB
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point established in this paper as a PET-coupled control step; not explicitly designated rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Impaired PPX function under lack of oxidized plastoquinone causes massive Proto accumulation and reduced chlorophyll biosynthesis; restored oxidized PQ availability lowers Proto and rescues chlorophyll accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPX function is compromised in ptox2 petB, causing >86-fold Proto accumulation. 2. DCMU prevents PQ over-reduction, decreases Proto accumulation, and restores chlorophyll levels, supporting a requirement for oxidized plastoquinone for PPX activity. 3. Oxyfluorfen inhibition of PPX phenocopies the ptox2 petB Proto-accumulation phenotype. 4. PPX protein abundance is similar across strains, indicating functional impairment rather than loss of protein accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (ptox2 petB, ptox2 pcy, rescue line); HPLC metabolite profiling of tetrapyrroles; chemical inhibition with oxyfluorfen and DCMU; immunoblotting of PPX protein
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: PSAD constitutive/strong promoter used only for PTOX2 complementation, not for PPX
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Proto accumulation causes phototoxicity, pale green/yellow phenotype, brown precipitate in medium, lowered chlorophyll and heme, and increased light sensitivity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Highlights PPX as a chlorophyll-biosynthesis engineering target whose activity depends on plastoquinone redox balance; engineering should consider photosynthetic electron transport status and oxidized PQ availability
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PPX content did not change markedly among genotypes; authors propose algal PPX is a thylakoid membrane-bound protoporphyrinogen IX/plastoquinone oxidoreductase with FAD mediating electron transfer to PQ. Paper refers to the gene as PPX, alias PPOX; C. reinhardtii contains a single PPX gene.
